explicit_text: I first felt part of a real movement in early 1960 during the Nashville sit-ins, especially after my first arrest in February. I was deeply involved in the civil rights movement, serving as chairman of SNCC and also on the board of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference. I participated in key events like Freedom Day in Selma in 1963 and led marches in 1965, including the Selma to Montgomery march where I was brutally beaten at the Edmund Pettus Bridge. Throughout, I maintained a close relationship with Martin Luther King Jr. and worked alongside other leaders to push for voting rights and social change.

I participated in key events like Freedom Day in a small Alabama city in 1963 and led marches in 1965, including the march from that city to the state capital where I was brutally beaten at a famous bridge. Throughout, I maintained a close relationship with a prominent civil rights leader and worked alongside other leaders to push for voting rights and social change.

explicit_text: Nashville was where my civil rights activism really began, especially in the fall and winter of 1959 with nonviolent workshops and sit-ins at lunch counters downtown. It was there in late February 1960 that I was arrested for the first time, which made me realize I was part of a larger movement. The Nashville Student Movement was a key part of SNCC, and it connected young activists like me to the broader struggle for racial justice.

explicit_text: Selma was a focal point of the civil rights struggle, especially in the early 1960s when voter registration was almost nonexistent for black citizens. I first went there in 1963 for Freedom Day, where hundreds of blacks stood in line to register despite hostile law enforcement. In 1965, Selma became the site of major marches and protests, including the violent confrontation at the Edmund Pettus Bridge. The city was a symbol of resistance but also of the harsh repression faced by activists.

The city was a symbol of resistance but also of the harsh repression faced by activists.

explicit_text: Freedom Day in Selma on October 8, 1963, was a turning point in the civil rights movement. Hundreds of black citizens lined up at the county courthouse to take literacy tests for voter registration, but only a few were allowed in. We faced hostility from Sheriff Jim Clark and his deputies, and many were arrested. The event drew attention from figures like James Baldwin and Howard Zinn and underscored the urgent need for voting rights.

The event drew attention from figures like James Baldwin and Howard Zinn and underscored the urgent need for voting rights.

explicit_text: The civil rights movement was the larger struggle I dedicated my life to, starting in the late 1950s and early 1960s. It involved coordinated efforts like sit-ins, marches, and voter registration drives across the South, including Nashville and Selma. I worked within organizations like SNCC and SCLC, alongside leaders like Martin Luther King Jr., to challenge segregation and racial injustice. The movement faced violent opposition but made historic gains, especially in securing voting rights.

entity: March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom
explicit_text: The March on Washington in 1963 was a significant event where the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee called for 'one man, one vote.' It inspired us to take action in places like Selma to test that demand. The march helped galvanize the movement and brought national attention to the struggle for civil rights and economic justice.

explicit_text: I served as chairman of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee during the early 1960s, playing a key role in organizing protests and voter registration efforts. SNCC was deeply involved in Selma from 1962 onward and worked closely with the Nashville Student Movement. We sometimes had tensions with other groups like SCLC, but I worked to maintain unity. SNCC made tough decisions, like initially not participating officially in the Selma to Montgomery march, though I and a few others joined individually.
implicit_text: I served as chairman of the student nonviolent coordinating committee during the early 1960s, playing a key role in organizing protests and voter registration efforts. This committee was deeply involved in the Alabama city from 1962 onward and worked closely with the local student movement in the southern music city. We sometimes had tensions with other groups like the southern Christian leadership conference, but I worked to maintain unity. The committee made tough decisions, like initially not participating officially in the march from the Alabama city to the state capital, though I and a few others joined individually.

explicit_text: The bombing of a church in Birmingham in 1963, where four little girls were killed, was a tragic and galvanizing moment for the movement. It happened just weeks before Freedom Day in Selma and strengthened our resolve to fight for justice and voting rights. The horror of that event reminded us of the violent resistance we faced.

explicit_text: Sheriff Jim Clark was a vicious and mean law enforcement figure in Selma who fiercely opposed our efforts to register black voters. I encountered him multiple times, including during Freedom Day in 1963 and again in January 1965 when he called me an 'outside agitator' and had us arrested. His posse used violence and intimidation against peaceful protesters, making him a symbol of the resistance we faced.

explicit_text: James Baldwin was one of the outside observers who came to Selma during Freedom Day in 1963. His presence, along with others like Howard Zinn, helped bring national attention to the struggle for voting rights. Baldwin's role as a writer and intellectual gave voice to the movement's cause beyond the immediate protests.

explicit_text: Martin Luther King Jr. was my hero and mentor from the time I was a teenager growing up in Alabama. He invited me to join the board of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference in 1962, and I served as a liaison between SCLC and SNCC. Though we sometimes had differences, he was like a big brother and symbol of the movement. I worked closely with him throughout the civil rights struggle, especially in Selma.

explicit_text: The Selma to Montgomery marches in 1965 were pivotal in the fight for voting rights. After the killing of Jimmie Lee Jackson, leaders including Andrew Young, Hosea Williams, James Bevel, and I organized a peaceful, nonviolent march covering fifty miles. On the first day at the Edmund Pettus Bridge, we were met with violent resistance from Alabama state troopers and Sheriff Clark's posse. Despite being beaten and hospitalized, the marches helped bring national attention and federal protection for voting rights.

Despite being beaten and hospitalized, the marches helped bring national attention and federal protection for voting rights.

explicit_text: Jimmie Lee Jackson was a young black man from Marion, Alabama, who was shot and killed during a peaceful protest in early 1965. His death deeply affected us and became a catalyst for the Selma to Montgomery marches. He was leading a demonstration in Perry County when he was shot and later died at a hospital in Selma.

This tragic event in Perry County helped spark the larger Selma to Montgomery voting rights campaign.

explicit_text: Atlanta was the site of an all-night SNCC executive committee meeting in March 1965 where we debated whether to participate in the Selma to Montgomery march. The decision was made that SNCC would not officially join, but I and three others chose to go as individuals. Atlanta was also where I met with other leaders to plan the march and coordinate efforts.

explicit_text: The Edmund Pettus Bridge in Selma was the site of a brutal confrontation on March 7, 1965, when peaceful marchers, including myself, were attacked by Alabama state troopers and Sheriff Clark's posse. We stood silently and peacefully as the troopers advanced with clubs, tear gas, and horses. I was beaten and hospitalized after the violence, but the event became a powerful symbol of the civil rights struggle.

explicit_text: The Alabama State Troopers were the law enforcement officers who violently confronted us during the Selma to Montgomery march at the Edmund Pettus Bridge in 1965. They used clubs, tear gas, and horses to beat and disperse peaceful protesters. Their actions were a stark example of the brutal resistance to civil rights in the South.

explicit_text: After being beaten during the Selma march at the Edmund Pettus Bridge, I was taken to Good Samaritan Hospital in downtown Selma. I was treated for a concussion and stayed there for three days. The hospital was where I recovered from the injuries inflicted by law enforcement during the protest.

explicit_text: During the Selma march in 1965, I questioned how President Johnson could send troops to fight in the Vietnam War while refusing to protect black citizens in Selma seeking their right to vote. This contradiction highlighted the government's misplaced priorities and the urgency of our cause. The war was a backdrop to the civil rights struggle, influencing our critique of American policies.

explicit_text: I referenced the Congo Crisis during the Selma march to point out the irony that the U.S. government was willing to send troops abroad to places like the Congo but not to protect black Americans fighting for voting rights at home. This comparison underscored the hypocrisy in U.S. foreign and domestic policies during the 1960s.
